doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Centrilobular emphysema in the lungs (predominantly in the upper lobes). The left hemidiaphragm is mildly elevated; on this background, the lower-basal portions of the left lung are compressed, with linear subsegmental atelectasis.

Linear atelectasis is also present in the right middle and lower lobes and in the left lingular segments.

In the lung parenchyma, single solid nodules are identified, in series 5 presented as follows:
No nodules requiring follow-up are identified in the expanded portions of the lungs.
No pleural effusion.
The trachea and central bronchi (except for the atelectasis described above) are patent.

Mediastinum and hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: Cardiac chambers are enlarged. No clinically significant pericardial effusion.
Aortic valve calcification. Severe coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta measures up to 4.1 cm, with wall calcification and S-shaped tortuosity of the descending segment. The main pulmonary artery measures up to 4.5 cm.

Osseous structures and chest wall:
Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation.
Decreased bone density in the vertebrae at the T11-L1 level of less than 100 HU (CT signs of osteoporosis). Mild rightward scoliosis of the thoracic spine with apex at the T10 level.

Included abdomen:
In the liver parenchyma, hypodense lesions with well-defined smooth margins and fluid density are present, the largest in segment 7 measuring up to 3.0 × 2.4 cm. Pancreatic lipomatosis.

Thyroid gland: In the left lobe, an indistinct heterogeneous hypodense lesion measures up to 1.1 × 0.9 cm; previously the visualization was unsatisfactory.
doc_impression:
1. No nodules requiring follow-up are identified in the expanded portions of the lungs. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria.

2. Dilated main pulmonary artery (4.5 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

3. Severe coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

4. Aortic valve calcification.

5. Atherosclerosis, aneurysmal dilatation, and S-shaped tortuosity of the thoracic aorta.

6. Cardiomegaly.

7. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Decreased vertebral body density consistent with osteoporosis at the T11-L1 level. Mild rightward scoliosis.

8. Hypodense liver lesions: Per Management of Incidental Liver Lesions on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee 2017, no CT follow-up is required.

9. Thyroid nodules: Per Managing Incidental Thyroid Nodules Detected on Imaging: White Paper of the ACR Incidental Thyroid Findings Committee 2014, thyroid ultrasound is recommended.

INDICATION:

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months:
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Mild centrilobular emphysema in the lungs (predominantly in the upper lobes). The left hemidiaphragm is mildly elevated; on this background, the lower-basal portions of the left lung are compressed, with linear subsegmental atelectasis. Linear atelectasis is also present in the right middle and lower lobes, in the left lingular segments. No nodules are identified. No pleural effusion. Thickening of the walls of segmental and subsegmental bronchi.

Mediastinum and hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy. Hiatal hernia. 

Heart: Cardiomegaly, cardiothoracic ratio is 0.66. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Aortic valve calcification. Severe coronary calcification. 

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta measures up to 4.1 cm, with wall calcification and S-shaped tortuosity of the descending segment. The main pulmonary artery measures up to 4.5 cm.

Osseous structures and chest wall:
Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation, diffuse ossification of the anterior longitudinal ligament. Mild rightward scoliosis of the thoracic spine with apex at the T10 level. Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T11 - 60 HU, T12 - 59 HU, L1 - 63 HU. Osteosclerotic lesion of T4 vertebral body measures 9 mm, unchanged. 

Included abdomen: In the liver parenchyma, hypodense lesions with well-defined smooth margins and fluid density are present, the largest in segment 7 measuring up to 3.0 × 2.4 cm, unchanged. Pancreatic lipomatosis.

Thyroid gland: In the left lobe, hypodense lesions measure up to 1.1 cm.
val_impression:
1. No nodules requiring follow-up are identified. 

2. Mild pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria. Thickening of the walls of segmental and subsegmental bronchi.

3. Dilated main pulmonary artery (4.5 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

4. Severe coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

5. Cardiomegaly. Aortic valve calcification. Atherosclerosis, dilatation, and S-shaped tortuosity of the thoracic aorta. 

6. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine, diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis. Decreased vertebral body density consistent with osteoporosis at the T11-L1 level. Mild rightward scoliosis.

7. Multiple hepatic cysts, unchanged. Per Management of Incidental Liver Lesions on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee 2017, no CT follow-up is required.

9. Thyroid nodules: ACR White Paper recommendations (Hoang, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2015;12:143-150) suggest the following: No need for further follow-up.

INDICATION: R91.8 other nonspecific abnormal findings of the lung field.

COMPARISON: Chest CT on February 11, 2025, March 19, 2024.

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 14 mGy; total DLP 533.23 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 1.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: In both lungs there are: multiple nodules measuring 1–2 mm in both lungs; discoid atelectasis in lung bases and anterior portions; mild ground-glass opacities in posterior portions, more likely dependent in etiology.  
In the right upper lobe, a previously not identified part-solid nodule measures up to 3 mm in diameter on image 76.  
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. Pericardial effusion with thickness up to 8 mm. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as severe.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. Calcified atherosclerotic plaques in the wall of the thoracic aorta. The main pulmonary artery is dilated up to 3.5 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Transpedicular spinal fixation. Position of the visualized hardware components is satisfactory. Degenerative changes of the thoracic spine and bilateral shoulder joints.

Upper abdomen: Gallstones in the gallbladder lumen, measuring up to 5 × 3 mm.
doc_impression:
1. Compared with June 11, 2025, previously not identified part-solid pulmonary nodule measuring up to 3 mm in diameter. Per 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, CT follow-up in 12 months.

2. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.4 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

3. Pericardial effusion.

4. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as severe. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

5. Micronodules in both lungs, not requiring follow-up.

6. Discoid atelectasis in both lungs.

7. Transpedicular spinal fixation.

8. Cholelithiasis.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Discoid atelectasis in lung bases and middle lobe and lingular segments and gravitational changes in dorsal lungs, clinically insignificant. In the right lower lobe there is a paravertebral area of osteophyte-induced pulmonary fibrosis, clinically insignificant. In the right upper lobe, a previously not identified part-solid nodule measures up to 4 mm in diameter on image 76, part-solid nodule measures up to 2 mm in diameter on image 78. In the right lung there are a few micronodules, unchanged. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. Pericardial effusion with thickness up to 8 mm. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as severe. 

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. Calcified atherosclerotic plaques in the wall of the thoracic aorta. The main pulmonary artery is dilated up to 3.5 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Transpedicular spinal fixation. Position of the visualized hardware components is satisfactory. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine and bilateral shoulder joints.

Upper abdomen: Gallstones in the gallbladder lumen, measuring up to 5 × 3 mm.

Thyroid: Both lobes of the thyroid gland are heterogeneous and enlarged.
val_impression:
1. Compared with June 11, 2025, previously not identified part-solid pulmonary nodules measuring up to 4 mm in diameter. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: Follow-up chest CT at 3-6 months to confirm persistence. If stable, consider chest CT at 2 and 4 years.

2. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.4 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension. Consider cardiac echo.

3. Small pericardial effusion.

4. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as severe. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

5. Cholelithiasis.

6. Heterogeneous and enlarged thyroid. Further evaluation with thyroid ultrasound is recommended if not already obtained elsewhere.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

INDICATION: Obstructive sleep apnea (OSA)G47.33, E11.9 type 2 diabetes and R07.1 chest pain on breathing.

COMPARISON: Chest CT on June 11, 2025.

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 14 mGy; total DLP 503.03 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 2.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Pulmonary nodules in series 5 are as follows:
Right upper lobe:
- Solid subpleural nodule measuring 5 mm on image 53 (unchanged).
In both lungs, micronodules measuring up to 3 mm. In both lungs, discoid atelectasis. In the left upper lobe, a calcified granuloma measuring up to 2 mm. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. Mild pericardial effusion measuring up to 6 mm in thickness. Coronary calcification is present in the left main, left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as severe. Aortic valve calcification.

Great vessels: Dilatation of the ascending thoracic aorta measuring up to 4.3 cm, with moderate wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is dilated measuring up to 3.2 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Demineralization of bone. Healing fractures of the left ribs. Spinal instrumentation at T1-T2.

Upper abdomen: In the left kidney, a mass with density of 8 HU, measuring 6.6 × 7.8 cm (unchanged).
doc_impression:
1. Solid nodule in the right upper lobe and micronodules in both lungs are stable compared with March 21, 2025 and September 05, 2025. In accordance with 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, additional CT follow-up is not recommended. Calcified granuloma in the left upper lobe is consistent with prior infection.

2. Mild pericardial effusion. Severe coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Aortic valve calcification.

3. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.2 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

4. Dilatation of the ascending thoracic aorta. Moderate atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

5. Demineralization of bone. Healing fractures of the left ribs. Spinal instrumentation at T1-T2.

6. Left renal mass. Per Management of the Incidental Renal Mass on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee, 2018, no further follow-up is required.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Pulmonary nodules in series 5 are as follows:
Right lower lobe:
- Solid nodule measures 2 mm on image 135 (unchanged).
- Previously identified on image 162 on Chest CT on March 21, 2025, solid nodule is resolved.
Left upper lobe:
- Calcified nodule measures 3 mm on image 67 (unchanged), calcified granuloma.
- Ground-glass nodule measures 3 mm on image 61 (unchanged).
Left lower lobe:
- Solid nodule measures 2 mm on image 69 (unchanged).
- Solid nodule measures 2 mm on image 90 (unchanged).
- Ground-glass nodule measures 3 mm on image 77 (unchanged).
In the right lower lobe, a group of micronodules (tree-in-bud) on images 157-169. Compared to Chest CT on March 21, 2025, resolution of peripheral ground-glass opacity is noted. Linear atelectasis in the right middle lobe, left lingula, and bilateral basal segments, gravity-dependent atelectasis. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. Trace pericardial effusion measuring up to 6 mm in thickness. Coronary calcification is present in the left main, left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as severe. Aortic and mitral valve calcification.

Great vessels: Dilatation of the ascending thoracic aorta measuring up to 4.3 cm, with moderate wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is dilated measuring up to 3.2 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Multiple healed rib fractures.

Upper abdomen: In the left kidney, a mass with density of 8 HU, measuring 6.6 × 7.8 cm (unchanged).
val_impression:
1. Solid and ground-glass nodules in both lungs are stable compared with March 21, 2025. For ground-glass nodules, ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: Follow-up chest CT at 2 and 4 years after the initial discovery of the nodules. For stable solid nodules, no further follow-up is recommended. Calcified granuloma in the left upper lobe is consistent with prior infection. Partially resolved bronchiolitis in the right lower lobe.

2. Severe coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

3. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.2 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

4. Trace pericardial effusion.

5. Dilatation of the ascending thoracic aorta. Moderate atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta. Aortic and mitral valve calcification.

6. Left renal cyst. Per Management of the Incidental Renal Mass on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee, 2018, no further follow-up is required.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_protocol:
INDICATION: Solitary pulmonary nodule.

COMPARISON: Chest CT on March 21, 2025, September 5, 2025.

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 20.72 mGy; total DLP 757.43 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 1.

doc_findings:
FINDINGS:
Lungs and pleura: Pulmonary nodules in series 5 are as follows:
Left upper lobe:
- A 6 mm nodule on image 99.
- A 4 mm nodule on image 94.
- A 2 mm nodule on image 89.
- A 2 mm nodule on image 145.
- A 2 mm nodule on image 143.
- A 2 mm nodule on image 88.
Right upper lobe:
- A 3 mm nodule on image 68.
- A 2 mm nodule on image 99.
Right middle lobe:
- A 2 mm nodule on image 142.
Air trapping in the posterior lungs. Calcified granulomas in the right lower lobe (numerous, clustered), and in the left upper and lower lobes (a few) measuring up to 4 mm. A single linear atelectasis in the right lower lobe. No pleural effusion on the right; small left pleural effusion.
Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass and no lymphadenopathy. Calcifications within a single right station 11 lymph node.
Heart: Moderately enlarged. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. No coronary calcification.
Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with a single focus of wall calcification.
Skeletal structures and chest wall: Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T11 - 136 HU, T12 - 123 HU, L1 - 115 HU. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Healing fracture of the right 4th rib. Enostosis in the head of the right humerus measuring up to 5 mm in diameter.
Upper abdomen: A lesion of the medial crus of the left adrenal gland measuring 8 mm × 12.0 cm, likely an adenoma. Multiple splenic calcifications measuring up to 4 mm. In liver segment 6, a calcification measuring 4 × 6 mm.
doc_impression:
1. Bilateral pulmonary nodules. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: For patients with low risk of lung cancer, initial chest CT at 3-6 months. If stable, no further follow-up or an optional chest CT at 18-24 months if there is high index of suspicion. For patients with high risk of lung cancer, initial chest CT at 3-6 months. If stable, another chest CT at 18-24 months.

2. Calcified granulomas and a single calcified lymph node (possibly related to a prior granulomatous process).

3. Small left pleural effusion.

4. Left adrenal lesion. ACR White Paper recommendations (Mayo-Smith, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1038-1044) suggest the following: Benign adenoma. No need for further imaging follow-up. Consider biochemical evaluation to determine functional status.

5. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

6. Moderate cardiomegaly.

7. Decreased vertebral body density (CT signs of demineralization).

8. Liver calcification.

9. Splenic calcifications.

10. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Pulmonary nodules in series 5 are as follows:
In the right upper lobe:
- A 3 mm solid nodule on image 68.
- A 2 mm solid nodule on image 99.
In the right middle lobe:
- A 2 mm solid nodule on image 142.
In the left upper lobe:
- A 6 mm solid nodule on image 99.
- A 4 mm solid nodule on image 94.
- A 2 mm solid nodule on image 89.
- A 2 mm solid nodule on image 145.
- A 2 mm solid nodule on image 143.
- A 2 mm solid nodule on image 88.
Numerous, clustered calcified nodules in the right lower lobe, and a few in the left upper and lower lobes measure up to 4 mm. Air trapping in the lower lobes. Gravitational changes in the dorsal lungs. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass and no lymphadenopathy. Calcifications within hilar and interlobar lymph nodes.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. Trace pericardial effusion with an effusion thickness of up to 8 mm. No coronary calcification.

Great vessels: The pulmonary arteries and thoracic aorta are of normal caliber. 

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T11 - 136 HU, T12 - 123 HU, L1 - 115 HU (CT signs of demineralization). Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Healing fracture of the right 4th rib. Enostosis in the head of the right humerus measuring up to 5 mm in diameter.

Upper abdomen: A lesion of the medial crus of the left adrenal gland measuring 8 mm (adrenal lesion <1 cm in size in the short axis does not require follow-up according to Management of Incidental Adrenal Masses: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee, 2017). Multiple splenic calcifications measuring up to 4 mm, benign. In liver segment 6, a calcification measuring 4 × 6 mm was reported on ultrasound in 2024, probably benign.
val_impression:
1. Multiple bilateral solid nodules up to 6 mm. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: 
- For patients with low risk of lung cancer, initial chest CT at 3-6 months. If stable, no further follow-up or an optional chest CT at 18-24 months if there is high index of suspicion. 
- For patients with high risk of lung cancer, initial chest CT at 3-6 months. If stable, another chest CT at 18-24 months.

2. Multiple calcified nodules and calcified mediastinal lymph nodes correspond to a prior granulomatous process.

3. Trace pericardial effusion.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_protocol:
INDICATION: R91.1 Solitary pulmonary nodule.

COMPARISON: None available.

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 6 mGy; total DLP 186 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 1.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Linear atelectasis in the right middle lobe, lingular segments, and basal segments bilaterally. Pulmonary nodules are present; on series 5, as follows:
In the right lung:
- In the upper lobe:
  - Subpleural solid nodule measuring up to 3 mm (image 112).
  - Two subsolid nodules measuring up to 4 mm and 3 mm, respectively (image 65).
Also in the upper lobe, a calcification measuring up to 3 mm is present (image 67).
In the left lung:
- In the lower lobe:
  - Perifissural nodule measuring up to 5 mm (image 114).
No pleural effusion.
The trachea and central bronchi are patent.
Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No masses or lymphadenopathy.
Heart: No cardiomegaly. Mild coronary calcification (right coronary artery). The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Pericardial fluid measures up to 1.1 cm in thickness.
Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated; wall calcification is present. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.
Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with forming osteophytes and Schmorl's nodes (at the L2, T12, and L1 levels). Decreased bone density in the vertebrae at the T11-T12 level to +118 HU, at the L1 level to less than +95 HU. Healing fractures of the ribs (anterior portions of the 7th and 8th ribs on the right, and the 10th rib on the left).
Upper abdomen: In the left kidney parenchyma (upper third), a lesion with well-defined smooth margins and fat density measures up to 9 × 6 mm.
Thyroid gland: In the left lobe, a hypodense lesion measures up to 6 mm in diameter, with well-defined smooth margins.
doc_impression:
1. CT appearance of a small number of pulmonary nodules of different density: small subsolid nodules in the right lung, small solid nodules in both lungs; per Fleischner Society recommendations (2017), no further CT follow-up is required.

2. Mild coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Moderate pericardial effusion.

3. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

4. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with forming osteophytes and Schmorl's nodes. Decreased bone density at the T11-L1 levels. Healing rib fractures bilaterally.

5. Fat-density hypodense lesion in the left kidney may correspond to angiomyolipoma; per Management of the Incidental Renal Mass on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee (2018), CT follow-up is not required.

6. Thyroid nodules; per Managing Incidental Thyroid Nodules Detected on Imaging: White Paper of the ACR Incidental Thyroid Findings Committee (2014), no further evaluation is required.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Pulmonary nodules are present; on series 5, as follows:
In the right upper lobe:
- Two ground-glass nodules measure up to 4 mm and 3 mm (image 65).
- Calcified nodule measures up to 3 mm (image 67), healed granuloma, requires no follow-up.
In the right middle lobe:
- Subpleural solid nodule measures up to 3 mm (image 112).
In the left lower lobe:
- Perifissural nodule measures up to 5 mm (image 114), perifissural lymph node, requires no follow-up.
Linear atelectasis in the right lower lobe. No pleural effusion. The trachea and central bronchi are patent. Wall thickening of the subsegmental and segmental bronchi with secretions.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No masses or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. Hyperdense areas at the level of the right coronary artery. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Pericardial fluid measures up to 1.1 cm in thickness.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated; moderate wall calcification is present. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with forming osteophytes and Schmorl's nodes (at the L2, T12, and L1 levels). Decreased bone density in the vertebrae at the T11-T12 level to +118 HU, at the L1 level to less than +95 HU (CT signs of demineralization). Healed fractures of the ribs (the 7th and 8th ribs on the right, and the 10th rib on the left).

Upper abdomen: In the left kidney parenchyma (upper third), a lesion with well-defined smooth margins and fat density measures up to 9 × 6 mm.

Thyroid gland: In the left lobe, a hypodense lesion measures up to 6 mm in diameter.
val_impression:
1. In the right upper lobe, two ground-glass nodules. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: Follow-up chest CT at 3-6 months to confirm persistence. If stable, consider chest CT at 2 and 4 years. In the right middle lobe, subpleural solid nodule measures up to 3 mm; in case of high risk, optional CT follow-up after 12 months. Wall thickening of the subsegmental and segmental bronchi with secretions may represent bronchitis.

2. Hyperdense areas at the level of the right coronary artery may be calcifications. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

3. Moderate pericardial effusion.

4. Fat-density hypodense lesion in the left kidney may correspond to angiomyolipoma; per Management of the Incidental Renal Mass on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee (2018), CT follow-up is not required.

5. Thyroid nodules; per Managing Incidental Thyroid Nodules Detected on Imaging: White Paper of the ACR Incidental Thyroid Findings Committee (2014), no further evaluation is required.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
